title: Court Proceedings – A Practical Guide
description: Types of court proceedings, how civil cases work, timelines, court costs, and appeal options. A practical guide for those dealing with the court system.
content:
Types of Civil Proceedings

Polish civil procedure covers a range of different types of proceedings tailored to the nature of each case. Understanding the basic types helps clarify what to expect at each stage and how to prepare.

  • Ordinary (adversarial) proceedings – the most common mode for resolving civil disputes, including claims for payment, damages, and property rights. Involves an exchange of pleadings, hearings, evidence-taking, and a judgment.
  • Order-for-payment proceedings (nakazowe/upominawcze) – simplified procedures for pursuing monetary claims in which the court issues a payment order without scheduling a hearing. Fast and cost-effective for the claimant.
  • Non-contentious proceedings – used in matters without a dispute between parties, such as confirming inheritance, estate division, incapacitation, and company registration.
  • Commercial proceedings – a separate track for disputes between businesses, with stricter procedural requirements and shorter timeframes.
  • Mediation – an alternative to court proceedings; a mediator helps the parties reach an agreement, and an approved settlement has the force of a court judgment.

How Does a Typical Civil Case Work?

Standard adversarial proceedings consist of several stages:

  1. Filing the statement of claim – the claimant submits the claim to the competent court, pays the court fee, and sets out their claim.
  2. Service on the defendant – the court serves a copy of the claim on the defendant, who has a set period to respond.
  3. Exchange of pleadings – the parties exchange written submissions, file motions for evidence, and submit documents.
  4. Hearing – the parties (or their lawyers) present their arguments; the court conducts evidence-taking, hears witnesses and experts.
  5. Judgment – the court delivers a judgment, which becomes enforceable once final.

Timeframes and Duration of Cases

The duration of court proceedings depends on court workload, case complexity, and the conduct of the parties. In practice:

  • Payment order proceedings – issuing an order: days to a few weeks.
  • Commercial proceedings at first instance – from several months to over a year.
  • Civil cases with multiple witnesses or experts – potentially several years, especially when appeals reach the Court of Appeal.

A skilled lawyer can accelerate proceedings by carefully preparing pleadings and evidence motions from the very beginning.

Costs of Court Proceedings

The costs of litigation include:

  • Court fee on the claim – depends on the value of the dispute or type of case (from PLN 30 in simplified proceedings to 5% of the claim value in other cases).
  • Lawyer's fees – the fee agreed with the law firm for representation.
  • Expert and interpreter costs – if specialist opinions are required.
  • Costs of legal representation – if you lose, the court may order you to pay the winning party's legal costs.

Appeals – What If the Judgment Is Unfavourable?

Polish civil procedure provides several means of appeal:

  • Appeal (apelacja) – against a first-instance court judgment to the court of appeal; deadline 14 days from service of the judgment with reasoning.
  • Interlocutory appeal (zażalenie) – against court orders issued during proceedings; deadline 7 days.
  • Cassation appeal (skarga kasacyjna) – against a court of appeal judgment to the Supreme Court; available subject to additional conditions.

Specter & Associates Law Firm in Warsaw provides comprehensive support at every stage of court proceedings – from preparing the statement of claim to representation before the Court of Appeal. Contact us to discuss your case.
author: Harvey Specter
